skill.name: deploy-model
skill.description: Guides Azure OpenAI model deployment in Azure AI Foundry with routing for quick presets, custom settings, and capacity discovery.
skill.summary: Deploy Azure OpenAI models with project confirmation, quota checks, and deployment-mode routing.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the confirmed high findings are false positives from Markdown fences, PowerShell line continuations, and non-cryptographic text. The skill is still medium risk because it guides cloud model deployment and includes helper scripts that run local commands and generate Azure portal URLs. No evidence found of prompt injection or malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Resource Deployment Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill is intended to guide Azure OpenAI deployment workflows and asks the assistant to confirm projects before deployment. This is legitimate DevOps behavior, but it can create or modify cloud resources and may incur cost if used incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes deployment workflows, Azure CLI prerequisites, project confirmation, SKU validation, and quota checks. The workflow appears legitimate, but it affects Azure resources.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Utilities Used In URL Helper
medium_findings.description: The Bash helper uses command substitution to transform a subscription ID with local utilities. Inputs are quoted and there is no eval call or network request, so this is not command injection, but running helper scripts remains an execution risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static finding is real local command execution through echo, tr, xxd, and base64. The context shows encoded URL construction rather than malicious execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Azure Portal URL Is Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The scripts contain a fixed ai.azure.com portal URL and print a generated deployment link. No evidence found that either script sends data over the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a Microsoft Azure portal destination, and the code returns or echoes the link instead of making an HTTP request.

low_findings.title: PowerShell Backticks Are Line Continuations
low_findings.description: The PowerShell backticks flagged as shell execution are used to continue a function call across lines. No evidence found of PowerShell expression execution or command injection at those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is a standard PowerShell line continuation for named parameters. It does not execute interpolated code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Documentation Blocks
low_findings.description: Most backtick findings in SKILL.md and TEST_PROMPTS.md are Markdown fences, inline literals, or prompt examples. No evidence found that these lines execute code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is rendered documentation or example CLI syntax. It is not embedded executable skill code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The lines flagged for weak cryptography contain descriptive text such as OpenAI references, headings, and deployment guidance. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of each flagged line shows prose, not cryptographic API usage. The static labels are not supported by semantic context.

low_findings.title: Relative Cross-Skill Link Is Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding is a Markdown link to a sibling quota skill. It may create a documentation dependency outside this folder, but it does not read files or perform filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path appears only inside a Markdown link. There is no code that opens or writes the referenced path.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Test Prompt Is An Input Example
low_findings.description: The region validation prompt is an edge-case test for invalid deployment regions. No evidence found that TEST_PROMPTS.md instructs unauthorized reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of a negative test table and expects graceful failure for an invalid Azure region. It does not request host, network, or credential discovery.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution In Bash Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The Bash helper executes local transformation commands through command substitution. The usage is bounded to URL generation, but reviewers should keep script execution visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present and execute locally, but the arguments are quoted and the output is only a portal URL.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Commands In Deployment Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs use of Azure CLI commands to inspect model SKUs and subscription usage. These commands are read-oriented in this file, but they support a larger deployment workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete external CLI examples. They query Azure metadata and quota rather than performing deployment directly.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded External Portal Domain
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts embed the Azure AI Foundry portal domain. This is expected for URL generation and does not make a network connection by itself.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded domain is visible in both scripts. Context shows portal-link construction, not data transfer.

content.value_statement: Azure OpenAI deployments require correct project, region, SKU, capacity, and quota choices. This skill routes requests to preset, custom, or capacity workflows and keeps project confirmation visible.
actual_capabilities: Routes deployment requests to preset, custom, or capacity discovery workflows.
Requires confirmation of the Azure project and region before deployment.
Guides checks for model SKU support through Azure CLI catalog queries.
Guides quota checks before presenting deployment capacity options.
Includes helper scripts that generate Azure AI Foundry deployment URLs.
Documents negative routing cases for delete and list deployment requests.
limitations: It depends on Azure CLI authentication and valid Azure permissions.
It does not list or delete existing deployments.
It relies on linked sub-skills for preset, custom, and capacity details.
It cannot guarantee capacity because Azure quota and regional availability change.

use_cases.title: Deploy a model quickly
use_cases.description: Use preset routing when a developer wants a model deployed with sensible defaults and project confirmation.

use_cases.title: Customize deployment settings
use_cases.description: Use custom routing when a platform engineer needs version, SKU, capacity, or content filter control.

use_cases.title: Find available capacity
use_cases.description: Use capacity routing when an operations team needs regions and projects with enough quota before deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy gpt-4o to my Azure AI Foundry project with the recommended defaults. Confirm the target project first.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Deployment Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me deploy o3-mini and choose the model version, SKU, capacity, and content filter policy.

prompt_templates.title: Check Capacity First
prompt_templates.scenario: Capacity discovery before creating a deployment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find where I can deploy gpt-4o with 100K TPM, then show the best project and region options.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Controlled Rollout
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request combining quota, region, and confirmation controls.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a custom Azure OpenAI deployment for gpt-4o with quota validation, supported SKU checks, and final confirmation before any changes.

output_examples.output: The assistant identifies preset mode, confirms the Azure project and region, checks prerequisites, and asks for approval before deployment.

output_examples.input: Where can I deploy o3-mini with 200K TPM?
output: The assistant routes to capacity discovery.
It compares regions and projects with available quota.
It recommends viable options before any deployment step.

output_examples.output: The assistant starts a guided flow for version, SKU, capacity, and content filter choices, then confirms the final target.

best_practices: Confirm the Azure project, region, and subscription before deployment.
Validate model SKU support and quota before presenting options.
Use capacity discovery when the request includes throughput or region constraints.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy without showing the selected Azure project first.
Do not present hardcoded SKU lists without checking current catalog data.
Do not use this skill for listing or deleting existing deployments.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy Azure OpenAI models?
faq.answer: It guides deployment workflows and routes requests to preset, custom, or capacity paths. It requires confirmation before deployment.

faq.question: Does it require Azure CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill expects Azure CLI to be installed and authenticated with access to the target subscription.

faq.question: Can it choose the best region?
faq.answer: It can route capacity and region questions to discovery guidance, then recommend viable deployment locations.

faq.question: Can it customize SKU and capacity?
faq.answer: Yes. Custom mode covers version, SKU, capacity, and content filter choices.

faq.question: Can it list or delete deployments?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly excludes listing and deleting existing deployments.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill involves cloud resource deployment and includes helper scripts. Users should review targets and permissions before running actions.
